title: Magnetic Beads Cell Separation
author: Laura Sánchez
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.8schwaw

steps.title: Prepare the magnetic module
steps.instruction: Prepare the magnetic module: remove the magnetic plate and load the necessary number of 1.5-mL microcentrifuge tubes.

steps.title: Prepare fresh cultured E
steps.instruction: Prepare fresh cultured E. coli samples with cell concentrations of 10 6 CFU/mL in PBS:

steps.title: Add 1 mL of each sample...
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of each sample [2] to a new eppendorf. Add 300 μL of MagneHis™ [3] to the bacteria mixture. Make sure cells are resuspended properly by gentle mixing.

steps.title: Measure the optical density of the...
steps.instruction: Measure the optical density of the bacterial inoculum at 600 nm absorbance (OD 600)

steps.title: Dilute it until you get the...
steps.instruction: Dilute it until you get the correct concentrations based on the relation: 1 OD < >1E9 CFU/mL [1]. Collect sample C o

steps.title: Incubate at room temperature for 30,...
steps.instruction: Incubate at room temperature for 30, 45 and 60 min each under gentle continuous agitation to prevent the beads from settling.

steps.title: Insert the magnetic plate into the magnetic module
steps.instruction: Insert the magnetic plate into the magnetic module. Allow the tube to stand for 3 min for maximum recovery.

steps.title: Remove the supernatant with the pipette,...
steps.instruction: Remove the supernatant with the pipette, very carefully, and put it in a new tube. Collect sample C nb

steps.title: Add 1 mL of Wash Buffer...
steps.instruction: Add 1 mL of Wash Buffer (or PBS) and resuspend by inversion. Allow the tube to stand for 3 min into the magnetic module.

steps.title: Repeat the washing process (steps 6-7) twice
steps.instruction: Repeat the washing process (steps 6-7) twice. Collect sample C w1 , C w2

steps.title: Resuspend the MagneHis™ complex in 100 μL of Elution Buffer
steps.instruction: Resuspend the MagneHis™ complex in 100 μL of Elution Buffer. Mix briefly by inversion. Allow the tube to stand for 1’5 min into the magnetic module.

steps.title: Take the supernatant with the pipette...
steps.instruction: Take the supernatant with the pipette carefully and add it to a 900 μL LB eppendorf. * Imidazole is very aggressive to the cell membrane, but it is needed to break the interaction between histidine and MagneHis™, therefore a high dilution is performed to stop its effect. Collect sample C f

steps.title: Resuspend the beads in 100 μL LB
steps.instruction: Resuspend the beads in 100 μL LB. Collect sample C ne

steps.title: For spread plating, a 10-fold dilution...
steps.instruction: For spread plating, a 10-fold dilution series (until dilution 10-7) is prepared. Plate 100 μLfrom dilutions 10-1, 10-2 and 10-3 onto 3 different agar plates with the pertinent antibiotic. The plates are incubated at 37°C for 48 h. The colonies are numerated, and the result is expressed as CFU/mL [4].

steps.title: We have developed this protocol from...
steps.instruction: We have developed this protocol from reading the following bibliography: [1] Park J, Gasparrini A, Reck M, Symister C, Elliott J, Vogel J et al. Plasticity, dynamics, and inhibition of emerging tetracycline resistance enzymes. Nature Chemical Biology. 2017;13(7):730-736. doi: 10.1038/nchembio.2376 [2] Lim M, Lee G, Huynh D, Hong C, Park S, Jung J et al. Biological preparation of highly effective immunomagnetic beads for the separation, concentration, and detection of pathogenic bacteria in milk. Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces. 2016;145:854-861. [3] http://t-takaya.net/manual/Miltenyi_Biotec_130-090-485.pdf. [4] Walcher G, Stessl B, Wagner M, Eichenseher F, Loessner M, Hein I. Evaluation of Paramagnetic Beads Coated with Recombinant Listeria Phage Endolysin–Derived Cell-Wall-Binding Domain Proteins for Separation of Listeria monocytogenes from Raw Milk in Combination with Culture-Based and Real-Time Polymerase Chain Reaction–Based Quantification. Foodborne Pathogens and Disease. 2010;7(9):1019-1024. doi: 10.1089/fpd.2009.0475
